California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

April 15, 1991

George H. Eiser, III
City Attorney
1243 National City Boulevard
National City, CA 92050-4397

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-91-098

Dear Mr. Eiser:

This letter is in response to your request for advice
regarding the duties and responsibilities of National City Mayor
George H. Waters and Councilmembers Michael R. Dalla, Jessie E.
Van Deventer and Rosalie G. Zarate under the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act disqualify
Mayor George H. Waters and Councilmembers Michael R. Dalla, Jessie
E. Van Deventer and Rosalie G. Zarate from participating in
decisions regarding amendments to the National City downtown
redevelopment project redevelopment plan?

CONCLUSION

The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act disqualify
Mayor Waters and Councilmembers Dalla, Van Deventer and Zarate
from participating in decisions regarding amendments to the
National City Downtown Redevelopment Project Redevelopment Plan if
it is reasonably foreseeable that such decisions will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally, on their financial interests as discussed below.

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

File No. A-91-098
Page 2


FACTS

National city is located in southern San Diego County. The
city has a population of approximately 55,000 and is 8.55 square
miles in area. The city's land area is approximately 7.34 square
miles, or 4,697 acres in area. The city is bounded by the city of
San Diego on the north, the City of Chula Vista on the south, the
Pacific Ocean on the west, and the County of San Diego on the
east.

National city is a general law city with a directly-elected
mayor and four councilmembers. The current city council is
comprised of Mayor George H. Waters, Vice-Mayor Ralph Inzunza,
Councilman Michael R. Dalla, Councilman Jessie E. Van Deventer and
Councilwoman Rosalie G. Zarate.

Pursuant to the Community Redevelopment Law, Health and
Safety Code Section 33000 et seq., there is in each community a
public body known as the redevelopment agency of the community.
(Health and Safety Code Section 33100.) The redevelopment agency
in each community is "activated" by enactment of an ordinance by
the legislative body declaring that there is a need for an agency
to function in the community. The Redevelopment Agency of
National city was activated on April 11, 1967, pursuant to
Ordinance No. 1167. On October 14, 1975, the Community
Development Commission of National city, a combined redevelopment
agency and housing authority, was formed pursuant to Ordinance No.
1484. Also pursuant to Ordinance No. 1484, the city council of
National city declared itself to be the Community Development
Commission of National City.

Beginning in 1969, a series of redevelopment projects were
adopted in the community, namely, the E. J. Christman Business and
Industrial Park I Redevelopment Project; the E. J. Christman
Business and Industrial Park Redevelopment Project, Amendment
No. II; the South Bay Town and Country Redevelopment Project; and
the Center City Redevelopment Project. These projects were merged
with additional land to form the National City Downtown
Redevelopment Project, pursuant to Ordinance No. 1762, adopted on
December 11, 1981. Minor amendments to the redevelopment plan,
which added a total of approximately three acres to the project,
were approved by Ordinance No. 1821, adopted on May 22, 1984,
(Amendment I), and by Ordinance No. 1851, adopted on April 16,
1985 (Amendment II). With the 1984 and 1985 amendments, the
National City Downtown Redevelopment Project consists of
approximately 2,083 acres and constitutes approximately 44% of the
city's land area.

There are 712 commercial and industrial buildings in the
redevelopment project area. National City has 6,776 single family
residences and 8,280 multiple family residences. Of these, it is
estimated that approximately 4,525 single family residences, which
constitute 67% of all single-family residences in the city and
5,550 multi-family units, which constitute 67% of all multi-family

File No. A-91-098
Page 3


units in the city, are within the redevelopment project area. In
addition, you have determined that there are 1,782 businesses with
sales tax permits within the redevelopment project area. This
number represents 78% of all businesses with sales tax permits in
the city.

The city council is at present considering a new
redevelopment plan amendment commonly known as Amendment III.
This amendment is intended to accomplish two primary objectives:
(1) to amend the duration of the redevelopment plan, the limit on
tax allocations, and the limit on bonded indebtedness of the
existing plan; and (2) to allow payment by the Community
Development Commission of part of the cost of acquiring land for
and construction of a new police facility.

Specifically, the proposed amendment would provide for the
following:

a. Increasing the taxes which may be divided and allocated
from $58 million to $250 million.

b. Increasing the amount of bonded indebtedness which can be
outstanding from $18.2 million to $100 million.

c. Extending the duration of the provisions, requirements,
and controls of the redevelopment plan from the year 2005 to the
year 2015.

The financial objectives of the proposed amendment are the
following:

a. Pay debt service on existing bonds.

b. Make payments to other taxing agencies such as the County
of San Diego, the National School District, and the Sweetwater
Union High School District, probably through "pass-through"
agreements.

c. Set aside 20% for low income housing.

d. Payment of overhead and administrative expenses.

e. Allocation of the remaining funds one-half to
neighborhood improvement and one-half to commercial and industrial
development.

Additionally, the Community Development Commission would pay
for part of the cost of the value of land and of construction of
the building to be erected for the new police facility. The
amendment would not affect siting of the facility which has
already been determined.

The proposed amendment does not designate any specific sites
within the redevelopment project area for the expenditure of funds

File No. A-91-098
Page 4


accruing from the sale of bonds which would be made possible by
the amendment. The proposed amendment would not result in the
addition of territory to the redevelopment project area.

Financial Interests of National City Councilmembers

Mayor George H. Waters

     Mayor Waters owns the following interests within National
City and within the boundaries of the project area of the National
City Downtown Redevelopment Project Redevelopment Plan. None of
Mayor Waters' properties is located within 2,500 feet of the
proposed new police facility site.

     1.  A fee interest in real property located at 1436 Highland
Avenue. The fair market value of the property is greater than
$100,000. The property is improved with a commercial building
which is rented to Dr. Rudolfo Picazo, a dentist, producing an
annual income to Mr. Waters of between $1,000 and $10,000. The
lease to Dr. Picazo ends on December 31, 1991, and is renewable
for an additional one-year term upon mutual agreement.

     2.  A fee interest in real property located at 1440 Highland
Avenue. The fair market value of the property is greater than
$100,000. The property is improved with a residence, which is
rented, producing annual income of between $1,000 and $10,000.

     3.  A fee interest in real property located at 1442 Highland
Avenue. The fair market value of the property is greater than
$100,000. The property is improved with a commercial building,
which is rented to Maniflo Money Exchange, a money exchange which
buys and sells foreign currency, producing annual income to Mr.
Waters of between $1,000 and $10,000. The lease to Maniflo Money
Exchange ends on December 31, 1991, and is renewable for an
additional one-year term upon mutual agreement.

     4.  A fee interest in real property located at 1444-1446
Highland Avenue. The fair market value of the property is greater
than $100,000. The property is improved with a commercial
building, which is used as the place of business for a commercial
office machines sales and service business of which Mayor Waters
is the proprietor.

Councilmember Michael R. Dalla

     Councilmember Michael R. Dalla owns a personal residence at
1020 "C" Avenue, acquired in 1974, which is within National City
and within the boundaries of the redevelopment project area. This
property is located beyond 300 feet but within 2,500 feet of the
proposed new police facility site.

Councilmember Jessie E. Van Deventer

     Councilmember Jessie E. Van Deventer owns the following
interests within the City of National City and within the
boundaries of the project area of the National City Downtown
Redevelopment Project Redevelopment Plan. Councilmember Van

feet of the proposed new police facility site.

     1. A fee interest in real property at 110 East 16th Street.
The fair market value of the property is greater than $100,000.
The property is improved with a commercial building, which is
rented to Thompson Lacquer, a retailer of auto finish products,
producing an annual income to Mr. Van Deventer in excess of
$10,000. The term of the lease is from February 15, 1989, to

     2. A fee interest in real property at 34 East 17th Street.
The fair market value of the property is greater than $100,000.
The property is improved with a commercial building. Suite A is
rented to Manuel Acuna, an import auto parts store, producing an
annual income to Mr. Van Deventer in excess of $10,000. The term
of the lease is from November 1, 1990, to October 31, 2000.
Suites B and C are rented to Alba Rodriguez, who operates a party

produces an annual income to Mr. Van Deventer in excess of

     3. A fee interest in real property at 106 East 17th Street.
The fair market value of the property is greater than $100,000.
The property is improved with a commercial building which is
rented to Dobson Welding, a welding shop, producing an annual
income to Mr. Van Deventer in excess of $10,000. The term of the
lease is from October 1, 1990, to September 30, 2000.

     4. A fee interest in real property at 25 East 17th Street.
The fair market value of the property is greater than $100,000.
The property is improved with a commercial building which is
rented to Bush Power Brake Service, a truck and automotive brake
repair business, producing an annual income to Mr. Van Deventer in

     5. A fee interest in real property at 115 East 17th Street.
The fair market value of the property is greater than $100,000.
The property is improved with a commercial building. Suite A is
rented to Brake World, a truck and automotive brake repair
business, producing an annual income to Mr. Van Deventer in excess
of $10,000. The term of the lease is from April 15, 1990, to
April 14, 1993. Suite B is rented to Safelite Glass, an auto

glass service and repair business, producing an annual income to
Mr. Van Deventer in excess of $10,000. The term of the lease is
from September 1, 1988, to August 31, 1993. Suite C is rented to
Trini's Auto Electric, an auto electric repair business, producing
an annual income to Mr. Van Deventer of approximately $10,000.
The term of the lease is from September 15, 1988, to September 14,

     8. A fee interest in real property at 115 East 18th Street.
The fair market value of the property is greater than $100,000.
The property is improved with a commercial building, which is
rented to Prochem Specialty Products, a wholesaler of cleaning
solutions, producing an annual income to Mr. Van Deventer in
excess of $10,000. The term of the lease is from November 1,
1990, to October 31, 1993.

     In addition to the above real property interests,
Councilmember Van Deventer has the following financial interests
in the jurisdiction:

     1. During 1989 Wirtz Title Company, Inc. was granted
authority to draw upon a line of credit held in the name of
Mr. Van Deventer and his wife at San Diego National Bank. The
total draw against said line of credit was approximately $75,000
during 1989. Wirtz Tile makes all payments due to San Diego

Title Company, Inc. was $2,000. Wirtz Title Company, Inc. is
located at 1533 Wilson Avenue, National City, within the

redevelopment project area but beyond 2,500 feet of the proposed
new police facility site.

2. Mr. Van Deventer is the beneficiary of a note with a
current balance of approximately $5,000, secured by a second deed
of trust on a residence located at 2965 Ridgeway Drive, National
City, which is outside the boundaries of the redevelopment project
and beyond 2,500 feet of the proposed new police facility site.
The trustor under the deed of trust and obligor on the note is
Juan Lopez, who owns an automotive machine shop at 1526 East 18th
Street, National City, located within the redevelopment project
area but beyond 2,500 feet of the proposed new police facility
site. Mr. Van Deventer receives a monthly payment of $108 under
the note.

3. Mr. Van Deventer is the obligee under a promissory note
in the amount of $52,178.56 executed by Nancy Thompson, owner of
three child care facilities in National City. The child care
facilities are located at 2207 Highland Avenue, 2200 "E" Avenue,
and 1540 Harbison Avenue, National City. The Highland Avenue and
"E" Avenue locations are within the boundaries of the
redevelopment project area, but beyond 2,500 feet of the proposed
new police facility site. The Harbison Avenue location is outside
the redevelopment project area and beyond 2,500 feet of the
proposed new police facility site. Mr. Van Deventer received
approximately $3,500 in payment of this note in 1990.

4. Mr. Van Deventer is the obligee under a promissory note
in the amount of $11,889.55 executed by Eugenio Trinidad, also a
lessee of Mr. Van Deventer's and the operator of an auto electric
repair business at 115 East 17th Street, Suite C, National City.
This location is within the redevelopment project area and beyond
300 feet but within 2,500 feet of the proposed new police facility
site. This note is secured by the inventory of Mr. Trinidad's
business. Mr. Van Deventer received $753.61 in income under this
note in 1990.

Councilmember Rosalie G. Zarate

Councilmember Rosalie G. Zarate owns the following interests
in real property within National City, and within the boundaries
of the project area of the National City Downtown Redevelopment
Project Redevelopment Plan. Neither of Councilmember Zarate's
properties is located within 2,500 feet of the proposed new police
facility site.

1. A fee interest in real property at 1735 Plaza Boulevard,
acquired in 1978. The fair market value of the property is
greater than $100,000. The property is improved with an apartment
building which is rented to individual tenants on month-to-month
tenancies, producing an annual income of over $10,000.

2. A personal residence located at 1730 East 12th Street,
acquired in 1964.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in, or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest.

An official has a financial interest in a decision within the
meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on the official or on a
member of the official's immediate family,2 or on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

(b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

(d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

(e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by, or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.

For purposes of this section, indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official, by an agent on behalf of a

___________________________
2 An official's "immediate family" are his spouse and dependent
children. (Section 82029.)

File No. A-91-098
Page 9


     public official, or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official's agents,
spouse, and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest
or greater.

                          Section 87103.

     Members of the city council are public officials.  (Section
82048.)  Accordingly, they may not make, participate in making, or
attempt to use their official position to influence a governmental
decision if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable and
material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally, on their economic interests.

Foreseeability

     The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur.  To be
foreseeable, the effect of a decision must be more than a mere
possibility; however certainty is not required.  (Downey Cares v.
Downey Community Development Comm. (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 983, 989-
991; Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal.App.3d 817; In re Thorner (1975)
1 FPPC Ops. 198 (copy enclosed.)  The Act seeks to prevent more
than actual conflicts of interest; it seeks to prevent even the
appearance of a possible conflict of interest.  (Witt v. Morrow,
supra at 823.)

Materiality

     Regulation 18702 (copy enclosed) sets forth the guidelines
for determining whether an official's economic interest in a
decision is materially affected by a decision.  If the official's
economic interest is directly involved in the decision, then
Regulation 18702.1 applies to determine materiality.  On the other
hand, if the official's economic interest is indirectly affected
by the decision, then Regulations 18702.2 to 18702.6 would apply
to determine whether the effect of the decision is material.

Public Generally

     Even if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
decision is material, disqualification is required only if the
effect of the decision on the official's economic interests is
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.  (Section
87103.)  For the city, the public consists of all residents of the
city.  Thus, disqualification is required unless the decision will
affect the councilmembers' economic interests in substantially the
same manner as it will affect all residents of the city or a
significant segment of the residents of the city.  (Regulation
18703, copy enclosed.)

     Applying these principles to your facts, we proceed with our
analysis of the financial interests you have presented for our

File No. A-91-098
Page 10


review.  You have stated that the purpose of the proposed
amendments to the redevelopment plan is to extend the duration of
the redevelopment plan, the limit on tax allocations, and the
limit on bonded indebtedness of the existing plan.  These
amendments to the redevelopment plan will translate into increased
revenues for the redevelopment project and an increase in public
improvements within the project area.  The amendments will also
prolong the duration of the redevelopment project and postpone the
prospective earnings to be realized from the expected increase in
property values at the completion of the redevelopment plan.
Therefore, it is foreseeable that the proposed amendments will
affect all interests in real property and other financial
interests within the redevelopment area.  If the effect is
material, as discussed below, disqualification is required unless
the "public generally" exception applies.

Please note that because siting of the police facility is not
at issue, distances from the councilmembers' real property
interests to the facility are irrelevant for purposes of our
analysis.  However, the decision to authorize the use of
redevelopment funds to pay for the police facility is a
governmental decision.  If there are circumstances which would
indicate that this decision will have a foreseeable and material
financial effect on the councilmembers' economic interests, then
you should apply the same analysis we discuss below to decisions
related to the use of redevelopment funds in partial payment for
the police facility.

Mayor George H. Waters

     a.  Real Property Interests

     Mayor Waters owns three commercial buildings within the
redevelopment area.  These buildings are leased to local
businesses who are sources of income to Mr. Waters in excess of
$250 in any 12-month period.  As a result of the proposed
amendments to the redevelopment plan, it is foreseeable that Mayor
Waters' commercial buildings will appreciate in value.  This is so
because the redevelopment project will receive increased
financing.  In fact, the proposed amendments contemplate the
allocation of funds to neighborhood improvement and commercial and
industrial development.  This increased financing will tend to
upgrade the redevelopment area and result in enhanced property
values.  If the effect of the decisions will be material,
disqualification is required.

     A decision is material if an official's real property
interests are directly involved in a decision and the decision
will have any financial effect on those interests.

File No. A-91-098
Page 11


An official's real property interest is directly involved in
a decision if:

     (D) The decision is to designate the survey
area, to select the project area, to adopt the
preliminary plan, to form a project area committee,
to certify the environmental document, to adopt the
redevelopment plan, to add territory to the
redevelopment area, or to rescind or amend any of
the above decisions; and real property in which the
official has an interest, or any part of it is
located within the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the redevelopment area.

     (E) For purposes of this subdivision, the
terms "zoning" and "rezoning" shall refer to the
act of establishing or changing the zoning or land
use designation on the subject property, but shall
not refer to an amendment of an existing zoning
ordinance or other land use regulation (such as
changes in the uses permitted, or development
standards applicable, within a particular zoning
category) which is applicable to all other
properties designated in that category.

                    Regulation 18702.1(a)(3)(D) and
                    (E), copy enclosed.

     If it is determined that Mayor Waters' interest in his
commercial buildings will be affected by any amount, Mayor Waters'
disqualification is required. The public generally exception does
not apply to Mayor Waters because we have no facts to indicate
that a significant segment of the population of the city owns
commercial buildings within the redevelopment area which would be
as singularly affected as Mr. Waters' interests. Therefore, we
conclude that his interests in the various commercial buildings
are disqualifying.

     Mayor Waters also owns a potentially disqualifying interest
in real property consisting of a single-family residence located
within the redevelopment area which is rented. This residence
will also be affected directly by the proposed redevelopment
decisions. As discussed above, even if the reasonably foreseeable
effect of a decision is material, disqualification is required
only if the effect of the decision on the public official's
economic interests is distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally or on a significant segment of the public. Your
facts indicate that 67 percent of all single-family residences in
the jurisdiction are situated within the redevelopment project.
This percentage constitutes a significant segment of the
population of the city which will be affected similarly by a

File No. A-91-098
Page 12


redevelopment decision. Thus, the "public generally" exception
would apply. We conclude that this residence is not a
disqualifying financial interest.

b. Sources of Income.

The tenants who lease office space in Mr. Waters' commercial
buildings, as well as the tenant in his single-family residence,
are sources of income in excess of $250 in a 12-month period to
Mr. Waters. Thus, these tenants, all located within the
redevelopment area, are potentially disqualifying sources of
income.

The tenants in Mr. Waters' commercial buildings are business
entities. These entities will be affected indirectly by
redevelopment decisions. When a business entity which is a source
of income to a public official is indirectly affected by a
decision, the applicable standard for determining materiality is
that of Regulation 18702.2 (copy enclosed). For all but the
largest business entities, Subdivision (g) of Regulation 18702.2
states that the effect of a decision is material if:

(1) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $10,000 or more; or

(2) The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or

(3) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of $10,000 or more.

Regulation 18702.2(g).

Additionally, these businesses have leasehold interests
within the redevelopment area. The appropriate standard for
determining materiality as applied to a leasehold interest in real
property is that of Regulation 18702.4 (copy enclosed). The
effect of a decision is material as to a leasehold interest in
real property if any of the following applies:

(a) The decision will change the legally
allowable use of the leased property, and the
lessee has a right to sublease the property;

(b) It is reasonably foreseeable that the
lessee will change the actual use of the property
as a result of the decision;

File No. A-91-098
Page 13


(c) It is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will result in a change in the actual use
of property within 300 feet of the leased property,
and the changed use will significantly enhance or
significantly decrease the use or enjoyment of the
leased property;

(d) The decision will increase or decrease
the amount of rent for the leased property by $250
or 5 percent, whichever is greater, during any 12-
month period following the decision; or

(e) The decision will result in a change in
the termination date of the lease.

Regulation 18702.4.

We are unable to determine whether the proposed decisions
regarding the amendments to the redevelopment plan will have the
effects listed as material in Regulation 18702.4 upon the
leasehold interests of tenants of Mr. Waters' commercial
buildings. In order to reach such a determination, a good faith
effort must be made to assess the impact of the decision on these
leasehold interests. For example, the lease to Maniflo Money
Exchange ends on December 31, 1991. Mr. Waters should make a good
faith effort to determine whether, as a result of the pending
decisions, the value of the lease will increase or decrease by
$250 in a twelve-month period. If he concludes that the lease
will be affected by this sum as a result of the decisions, the
effect of the decisions upon the lease is material and
disqualification is required.

If it is determined that decisions regarding the proposed
amendments to the redevelopment plan will affect the businesses
which are sources of income to Mr. Waters or the leasehold
interests in real property in the amounts stated above, Mr. Waters
must disqualify himself from participating in the decisions. For
example, it is foreseeable that extending the duration of the
redevelopment plan to the year 2005 will benefit these businesses.
This is so because once the redevelopment is completed, the value
of leases may increase as property values increase. Thus, the
cost of doing business in the redevelopment area will be higher.
If the estimated increase in the value of leases will be material,
so that term is defined in Regulation 18702.4, disqualification is
required.

Mr. Waters has a tenant who is a source of income in excess
of $250 in a single-family residence located in the redevelopment
area. If the effect of a redevelopment decision on this person
will be material, Mr. Waters must disqualify himself from
participating in redevelopment decisions. When an individual who
is a source of income to a public official is indirectly affected
by a decision, the appropriate standard for determining

File No. A-91-098
Page 14


materiality is that of Regulation 18702.6 (copy enclosed) which
states as follows:

     The effect of a decision is material as to an
     individual who is a source of income or gifts to an
     official if any of the following applies:

          (a)  The decision will affect the individual's
     income, investments, or other tangible or
     intangible assets or liabilities (other than real
     property) by $1,000 or more; or

          (b)  The decision will affect the individual's
     real property interest in a manner that is
     considered material under Section 18702.3 or
     Section 18702.4.

                         Regulation 18702.6.

     We have no information regarding this tenant.  This tenant
might own real property within the redevelopment area, or this
tenant might be doing business or employed in the redevelopment
area.  If it is determined that it is foreseeable that the effect
of redevelopment decisions regarding the proposed amendments will
affect this individual in the amounts stated above,
disqualification is required.

     c. Ownership Interest in a Business Entity

     Mr. Waters is also the proprietor of a business located
within the redevelopment area.  This business will be affected
indirectly by redevelopment decisions.  In order to determine
materiality, please refer to our discussion of Regulation 18702.2,
supra, and its applicability to business entities.

     In addition, clients of this business are sources of income
to Mayor Waters.  Any source of income in excess of $250 in the 12
months preceded the decision is potentially disqualifying.  We
discussed Mayor Waters' interest in his business and applicable
exceptions to the general rule in our advice to you, Our File No.
I-90-559 (copy enclosed).  At that time we indicated to you that
the retail customers exception contained in Section 87103.5 might
apply to these sources of income.  To assist you in making a
determination, we enclose Regulation 18703.5 which sets forth the
guidelines for exclusion of certain sources of income to a retail
business.

Councilmember Michael R. Dalla

     Councilmember Dalla's residence, situated within the
redevelopment area, is not a disqualifying interest in real
property.  This is so because you have advised us that 67% of all

File No. A-91-098
Page 15


single-family residences in the city are located within the
redevelopment area. This constitutes a significant segment of the
public. Thus, the public generally exception applies to
Councilmember Dalla who is not disqualified from participating in
redevelopment decisions.

Councilmember Jessie E. Van Deventer

a. Real Property Interests and Sources of Income

Councilmember Van Deventer owns numerous commercial buildings
within the redevelopment area which are leased to business
tenants. The analysis discussed above with reference to Mayor
Waters is also applicable to Councilmember Van Deventer's interest
in these buildings. Thus, for purposes of determining materiality
and disqualification, Mr. Van Deventer's buildings are directly
involved in the decisions and the standards for materiality are
those of Regulation 18702.1. Regulation 18702.2 should be applied
to determine materiality with respect to those businesses which
are tenants in Mr. Van Deventer's buildings and sources of income
to him in excess of $250 in the twelve months preceding a
decision. Additionally, Regulation 18702.4 should be applied to
determine whether the pending decisions will have a material
financial effect on the leasehold interests of the tenants in
these commercial buildings.

b. Ownership Interest in a Business

Additionally, Councilmember Van Deventer is the majority
stockholder in a closely held corporation which leases space in
one of Mr. Van Deventer's commercial buildings and which does
business within the redevelopment area.

The corporation will be affected indirectly by redevelopment
decisions. If it is reasonably foreseeable that the proposed
amendments will have a financial effect on the business,
disqualification is required if the effect will be material. When
a business entity will be affected indirectly by a decision, the
appropriate standard for determining materiality, as discussed
above, is that of Regulation 18702.2.

We have insufficient information to determine whether the
business conducted by the corporation will be affected by the
proposed amendments. However, also applicable to this business is
Regulation 18702.4 which determines materiality with respect to
the leasehold interest held by the business.

c. Economic Interest in Loans

By virtue of Mr. Van Deventer's financial relationship with
Wirtz Tile Company, Inc., Juan Lopez, Nancy Thompson and Eugenio
Trinidad, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the pending

Page 16


redevelopment decisions will have a material financial effect on
the businesses operated by these individuals within the
redevelopment area. Mr. Van Deventer must disqualify himself from
participating in such decisions. This is so because Mr. Van
Deventer has made loans to these parties which are still
outstanding. Therefore, these parties are sources of promised
income to Mr. Van Deventer. (Section 82030.) In order to
determine materiality with respect to these sources of income, the
appropriate standard is that of Regulation 18702.2 discussed
above. Additionally, you should apply Regulation 18702.1(a)(4) to
determine whether the pending decisions will affect Mr. Van
Deventer's economic interests in the sums stated in this
Regulation.

Councilmember Rosalie G. Zarate

Councilmember Zarate has an interest in an apartment building
which has at least four rental units. The applicable standard for
determining materiality in this instance is that of Regulation
18702.1 discussed above with reference to Mayor Waters' interests
in real property. If you determine that this interest in real
property is disqualifying pursuant to the provisions of Regulation
18702.1, Councilmember Zarate must disqualify herself from
participating in decisions regarding the proposed amendments to
the redevelopment plan unless the "public generally" exception
applies.

In In re Ferraro (1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 62, 67 (copy enclosed),
the Commission stated that a group that was large in numbers and
heterogeneous in quality constituted a significant segment of the
public for the purposes of the "public generally" exception.
Applying these principles, the Commission concluded that owners of
three or fewer residential rental units in the City of Los Angeles
constituted a significant segment of the public. Councilmember
Zarate owns one apartment building with more than three rental
units. Therefore, the "public generally" exception would not
apply to this interest in real property and her disqualification
is required.

Councilmember Zarate also has an interest in her residence.
We have insufficient information to determine whether this
residence is within the redevelopment area. However, as discussed
above, even if the reasonably foreseeable effect of a decision is
material, disqualification is required only if the effect of the
decision on the public official's economic interest is
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally or on a
significant segment of the public. Your facts indicate that 67%
of all single-family residences in the jurisdiction are located
within the redevelopment area. Therefore, even if this residence
is within the redevelopment area, the "public generally" exception
would apply to this interest in real property which is not
disqualifying.

embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission April 15, 1991 George H. Eiser, III City Attorney 1243 National City Boulevard National City, CA 92050-4397 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-91-098 Dear Mr. Eiser: This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the duties and responsibilities of National City Mayor George H. Waters and Councilmembers Michael R. Dalla, Jessie E. Van Deventer and Rosalie G. Zarate under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ QUESTION Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act disqualify Mayor George H. Waters and Councilmembers Michael R. Dalla, Jessie E. Van Deventer and Rosalie G. Zarate from participating in decisions regarding amendments to the National City downtown redevelopment project redevelopment plan? CONCLUSION The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act disqualify Mayor Waters and Councilmembers Dalla, Van Deventer and Zarate from participating in decisions regarding amendments to the National City Downtown Redevelopment Project Redevelopment Plan if it is reasonably foreseeable that such decisions will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on their financial interests as discussed below. 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 File No. A-91-098 Page 2 FACTS National city is located in southern San Diego County. The city has a population of approximately 55,000 and is 8.55 square miles in area. The city's land area is approximately 7.34 square miles, or 4,697 acres in area. The city is bounded by the city of San Diego on the north, the City of Chula Vista on the south, the Pacific Ocean on the west, and the County of San Diego on the east. National city is a general law city with a directly-elected mayor and four councilmembers. The current city council is comprised of Mayor George H. Waters, Vice-Mayor Ralph Inzunza, Councilman Michael R. Dalla, Councilman Jessie E. Van Deventer and Councilwoman Rosalie G. Zarate. Pursuant to the Community Redevelopment Law, Health and Safety Code Section 33000 et seq., there is in each community a public body known as the redevelopment agency of the community. (Health and Safety Code Section 33100.) The redevelopment agency in each community is "activated" by enactment of an ordinance by the legislative body declaring that there is a need for an agency to function in the community. The Redevelopment Agency of National city was activated on April 11, 1967, pursuant to Ordinance No. 1167. On October 14, 1975, the Community Development Commission of National city, a combined redevelopment agency and housing authority, was formed pursuant to Ordinance No. 1484. Also pursuant to Ordinance No. 1484, the city council of National city declared itself to be the Community Development Commission of National City. Beginning in 1969,
